coredata.dc:title: An innovative colorimetric platform for the low-cost and selective identification of Cu(II), Fe(III), and Hg(II) using GQDs-DPA supported amino acids by microfluidic paper-based (µPADs) device: Multicolor plasmonic patterns
coredata.dc:creator: Abdollahiyan, Parinaz
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Seidi, Farzad
Pashazadeh-Panahi, Paria
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the synthesis of functional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the thermal pyrolysis of D-penicillamine (DPA) and citric acid towards water treatment. For the first time, a novel type of highly fluorescent probe for selective Cu2+ detection among 26 types of metal ions which considerably enhance the fluorescence intensity of GQD. Furthermore, the recognition effects can be strengthen addition of amino acids owing to raise the electron density of the system and boos the accumulation profile of the sensing molecules. This simple fluorescent probe was applied for facile metal ions recognition in human urine samples, as well as environmental fluids. The proposed DPA-GQDs supported amino acids respond to Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+, with high sensitivity. The intensity of the fluorescence histogram of this probe significantly diminished in exposure to metal ions such as, Cu(II), Hg(II), and Fe(III). Moreover, in our work, microfluidic paper-based (µPADs) was fabricated through a facile and a cost-effective method. Cu2+ can selectively be recognized by GQD-DPA on the paper-based sensors by naked-eye. The functionalized DPA-GQDs is an excellent alternative to previously reported fluorescent probes for sensing, bio-labeling, and other biological usages in aqueous solution. Its nitrogen and oxygen-rich groups can coordinate with Cu2+ and cause to an apparent fluorescence quenching by the non-radiative electron-transmission. The technique presented in this paper is economical and simple in design, and provides a "mix-and-sense" protocol with no need to chemical- or dye-modifications. Our proposed Cu2+ sensing probe exhibits linear response in the concentrations ranging from 0.001 ppm to 5 ppm, with a lower limit of quantitation (LLOQ) of 0.1 ppm in both environmental fluids, and human urine samples. The enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Colorimetric sensing of the interaction between GQD-DPA, in the presence of different ions (Mg2+, Mo2+, B3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, As3+, Se4+, Cr3+, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, V3+, Cs+, Zr4+, W4+, K+, Li2+, Al3+, Na+, Si4+, and Mn2+) in standard solution, with 1 µg/ml concentration, by GQD-DPA solution (GQD-DPA/dilution buffer/ion are complexed with the ratio of 100 μl/200 μl/100 μl, respectively). As illustrated, it is obvious that Cu2+, Fe3+, and Pt4+ can be detected by GQD-DPA sensing solution.
GQD-DPA color alternation in the presence of different ions (Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Li2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+) to selectively detect Cu2+, and Hg2+, in standard solution, with 1 µg/ml concentration.

The fluorometry spectra of the interaction between GQD-DPA solution for detection of different ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Hg2+ and As3+), using standard solutions in aqueous sample (A), and the histogram of the adsorption intensity vs. types on ions (B).
Colorimetric detection of Fe3+, and Pt4+ by GQD-DPA using μPADs through capillary method under natural light.

Selectivity of Cu2+, and Hg2+ among the other metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Li2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+) (1 µg/ml), in the presence of GQD-DPA supported different amino acids (valine, proline, glycine, alanine, arginine, Leucine, cysteine, and methionine) with ratios of 200 μl for ions (1 µg/ml), 100 μl of amino acid solution (10 mM), and 200 μl of GQD-DPA.

Selectivity of Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+ in comparison with the control group (no ion treated GQD-DPA) in the presence of GQD-DPA supported different amino acids (valine, proline, glycine, alanine, arginine, Leucine, and cysteine) with ratios of 200 μl for ions (1 µg/ml), 100 μl of amino acid solution (1 M), and 200 μl of GQD-DPA.
Colorimetric detection of Fe3+, and Cu2+ by GQD-DPA supported cysteine using μPADs non-capillary method under natural light.

The general mechanism of the interactions between GQD-DPA and the metal cations (except Cu2+) (A), the mechanism of chemical interactions between GQD and DPA to synthesize GQD-DPA (B), and the mechanism of chemical interactions between GQD-DPA and Cu2+ ions, where DPA acts as a chelating agent for Cu2+ (C).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the synthesis of functional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the thermal pyrolysis of D-penicillamine (DPA) and citric acid towards water treatment. For the first time, a novel type of highly fluorescent probe for selective Cu2+ detection among 26 types of metal ions which considerably enhance the fluorescence intensity of GQD. Furthermore, the recognition effects can be strengthen addition of amino acids owing to raise the electron density of the system and boos the accumulation profile of the sensing molecules. This simple fluorescent probe was applied for facile metal ions recognition in human urine samples, as well as environmental fluids. The proposed DPA-GQDs supported amino acids respond to Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+, with high sensitivity. The intensity of the fluorescence histogram of this probe significantly diminished in exposure to metal ions such as, Cu(II), Hg(II), and Fe(III). Moreover, in our work, microfluidic paper-based (µPADs) was fabricated through a facile and a cost-effective method. Cu2+ can selectively be recognized by GQD-DPA on the paper-based sensors by naked-eye. The functionalized DPA-GQDs is an excellent alternative to previously reported fluorescent probes for sensing, bio-labeling, and other biological usages in aqueous solution. Its nitrogen and oxygen-rich groups can coordinate with Cu2+ and cause to an apparent fluorescence quenching by the non-radiative electron-transmission. The technique presented in this paper is economical and simple in design, and provides a "mix-and-sense" protocol with no need to chemical- or dye-modifications. Our proposed Cu2+ sensing probe exhibits linear response in the concentrations ranging from 0.001 ppm to 5 ppm, with a lower limit of quantitation (LLOQ) of 0.1 ppm in both environmental fluids, and human urine samples. The enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Atoms, molecules, or ions with positive or negative charges have in equal total amounts of protons and electrons that offer them a net negative or positive electric charge. Heavy metals possess high weights, high densities, and diverse atomic numbers (more than 20) [1,2]. Some of these elements are vital to human health in low dosages and harmful in high dosages. Furthermore, heavy metal ions perform an imperative role in industry, agriculture, and paper industries [3,4]. Moreover, their role in medicine and nutrition cannot be neglected. In fact, heavy metals (eg. mercury, cadmium, arsenic, and chromium) have affinity to thiol groups, and consequently, are able to link with the sulfur functional groups of enzymes in body [5,6]. This ability leads them to control the speed of metabolic reactions, and enzymes activities. Moreover, these metals threats human health since they are toxic when accumulate in human body [7,8]. For instance, mercury ion (Hg2+) is a hazardous element in body, which leads to skin cancer diseases when exceeds 0.002 ppm [9,10]. Therefore, developing sensitive methods for Hg2+ detection is essential for early diagnosis of Hg2+-related disorders. With this aim, researchers used electrochemical-based carbon quantum dot (CQD) modified electrode for the selective detection of Hg2+, with high sensitivity of 2.5 nM [11]. Likewise, in another study, a non-enzymatic electrochemical biosensor of CQD-modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes was used for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+, in low dosage of 0.5 nM [12]. Equally importantly, poly-pyrrole functionalized graphene/β-cyclodextrin composite was used as a sensing probe for electrochemical detection of Hg2+, with high sensitivity of 0.47 nM in environmental samples [13].

Iron is another significant element in environment, and most importantly, in humans’ bodies. Fe3+ is vital for creatures’ health, and its deficiency causes to several disorders such as bone weakening [14]. However, overloading of Fe3+ in tissue may cause adverse effects such as retinitis, thyroiditis, and conjunctivitis. Excessive concentrations of Fe3+ in environment may cause toxic effects in creatures’ health such as pneumoconiosis. Therefore, sensitive detection of iron in environmental and clinical samples is critical to prevent the relevant adverse side effects. For instance, researchers used iron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots as a sensing probe for Fe3+ detection in water through both colorimetric and fluorometric detection [15].

Copper is another vital element in body, causing to protect immune system, and make red blood cells. However, excessive concentrations of Cu2+ caused to several disorders such as kidney failure, Nausea, headache, and stomach pain [16]. One of the chief disorders associated with copper is Wilson. In this disease, ATP7B gene mutation disrupts copper transformation and consequently causes to over-accumulation of Cu2+ and toxic effects in brain and liver [17]. Poisoning caused by Cu2+ can lead to kidney disorders, renal failure, and headaches. Thus, this is a pivotal factor for enzymes and shows antioxidant properties [18,19]. Additionally, the striking role of ions in industry pollutions cannot be neglected. Water pollution is one of the most harmful phenomena caused by metal ions in industry [20]. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ is important to prevent the relevant toxic side effects. For instance, researchers used silver nanocapsules, with controlled size and shape, for selective detection of Cu2+ with high sensitivity of 2.6 × 10−9 mol L−1 [21]. In another study, a sensing system composed of mesoporous porphyritic metal-organic framework (PCN222) was used for selective detection of C2+ with LOD of 50 nM L−1 [22].

Regarding the importance of metal ions, in industry and the biomedical fields, expansion of sensitive and accurate sensing methods is in the central point of attention [4,23]. Recently, fluorescent assay reveals an easy, rapid response, accurate, and sensitive method which offers a better choice for recognition of metal ions [8,24,25]. Optical sensing probes based on GQDs possess a pivotal role in sensing of these ions because of their unique properties [26]. GQDs are semiconductor materials, which reveal variation of usages in medicine and industry. These nanoparticles possess unique features such as high stability, narrow width, and high electron transportation ability [27]. The photo-electronic characters of GQDs differ in size and shape. GQDs have been modified with various functional groups of diverse molecules or atoms show excellent potential for biochemical sensors applications [28–31]. The main aim of GQD functionalization is improving the electronic characteristics of GQDs that causing to provide a sensitive fluorescence response to detect a specific material. Researchers used functionalized GQD with DPA to improve the intensity of the florescence peak of GQD, owing to the decoration of GQD with the electron-riche amino and carboxyl groups. In their proposed system, Fe3+ showed admirable fluorescence response to GQD-DPA [32].

In this work, we report a GQD-DPA probe solution to enhance optical and fluorescence potential of GQD for ions detection both in the presence of amino acids, and on the bare state. Penicillamine containing carboxylic acid, thiol, and amine groups is functionally similar to cysteine. Thus, it can form numerous chemical bonds with several molecules [33]. This material is zwitterion at physiological pH and possesses two enantiomers, L-penicillamine inhibits pyridoxine (vitamin B6) and is toxic [34]. Though, D-penicillamine shows no cytotoxic effects in human cell lines. Herein, GQD-DPA was fabricated through hydrothermal reaction [35].

Recently, µPADs have demonstrated high attraction to be used in a variety of bioanalytical diagnostic and sensing sensors. µPADs fabrication usually needs hydrophobic barriers on the substrate to specify the fluid flows regions [36]. The hydrophilic nature of paper leads to induce the lateral flow action, and in consequent, the fluidic transport within the porous structure of the paper [37]. Colorimetric-based assay is one of the most favorable detection systems owing to admirable instrumental advantages such as portability and ease of detection by naked eye [38,39].

In this research study, we proposed µPADs with no need to any color-modifier reagents. Our µPADs works on the base of color alternation of the sensing probe solution to the paper, such that the light brown color of the GQD-DPA solution was changed to light orange in the presence of Cu2+, and Fe3+ on the paper, but the case is not the same for the other types of ions. Moreover, in this study, we employed GQD-DPA to detect Cu2+ and Hg2+ among other metal ions, selectively, resulting in noticeable alternations in optical and color properties, which was further confirmed through fluorescence spectroscopy.

Citric acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetone, and D-penicillamine (DPA) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Amino acids were obtained from Pubchem Company. The standard solutions of (Hg(II), Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) (with 1 μg/ml concentration) were purchased from chemlab company (Zedelgem, Belgium). All solutions were provided with deionized water. All of the used chemicals were of the highest purity or analytical grade. For all dilutions deionized water (pH = 7.4) was used. Paraffin was acquired from local market.

The size and morphology of the GQD-DPA particles were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by a Hitachi H-800 TEM device operated at a voltage of 200 kV. The spectra of absorption were recorded through UV–Vis spectroscopy, at room temperature, using a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence intensity and spectrum were recorded by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). Infrared spectra were recorded by a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometers. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was attained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using Siemens, D500 (Germany).

DPA-GQD was prepared by thermal pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid and D-penicillamine. First of all, D-penicillamine was extracted from capsules by acetone and purified in room temperature. Then, 0.29 g of D-penicillamine and 0.95 g of citric acid were dissolved in 10 ml deionized water. The mixture, was transferred into a 50 ml of autoclave pressure vessel. Flowed by heating at 200 °C for 2.5 h and cooling at room temperature to reach 24 °C. After preparation GQDs, successive dilution methods conducted to prepare transparent DPA-GQD solution. Approximately, 40 ml of DPA-GQD was diluted in 1000 ml deionized water in order to prepare 10 ml DPA-GQD (500 ppm).

The colorimetric identifications of ions were performed at room temperature. Briefly, different amino acids (ascorbic acid, arginine, alanine, cysteine, glycine, Leucine, methionine, valine) were diluted with deionized water, or human urine sample, separately, to obtain 1 M concentration of each amino acids, and combined with optical probe (GQD-DPA) with the ratio of (1:2, amino acid: GQD-DPA). Afterwards, the standard solutions of ions (Hg(II), Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) (with 1 μg/ml concentration) were added to the mixture solution of amino acid with GQD-DPA in the separate microtubes, the proportion of ion: GQD-DPA was considered as 1:1. Therefore, the standard solutions of different ions (1 ppm, 200 μl) were mixed with the mixture of 200 μl GQD-DPA solution and 600 μl buffer. Moreover different concentrations of Cu2+ (from 0.001 ppm to 5 ppm) were evaluated via colorimetric sensing, using diluted GQD-DPA as the sensing prob. The optical images were captured by Samsung note (II) android system camera.
This part of manuscript is similar to our previous report [9].
Colorimetric detection was performed with the camera (Samsung smart phone, 600-dpi resolution). The images were captured under flasher or natural light sources.

The fluorescent detection of the studied ions was carried out at room temperature using both bare GQD-DPA and amino acids-supported GQD-DPA. In a typical run, 1 M of different amino acids (valine, proline, alanine, arginine, and cysteine) was mixed with standard solutions of different ions (Cu2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, As3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, and Li+) (1 ppm) with the ratio of 200 μl of GQD-DPA, 100 μl of amino acids, and 200 μl of ions, and equilibrated at room temperature for 5 min, following by exposing to the fluorescence evaluation on the fluorescence spectrophotometer, repeating three time for each sample, the excitation wavelength was considered at 346 nm.

Concerning the importance of the toxic metal ions recognition for human and other creature's health, recently, various high-tech strategies are offered to detect toxic metal ions, both environmentally, and biologically [7]. With this aim, there are some traditional ways to detect these toxic materials [40] such as anodic stripping voltammetry [41], plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [42], and atomic absorption spectroscopy [43]. Despite the acceptable sensitivity and accuracy of these methods, there are still some limitations in terms of time-consuming processes, complicated sample treatments, and expensive instruments. Regarding to overcome the limitations of the traditional methods, modern methods are developed such as electronic and electrochemical analyses. However, these methods are also suffer low compatibility, instability, low selectivity, and complex on-site sampling. Hence, in order to have a highly sensitive, low cost, fast, simple, efficient, and selective method for toxic ions detection, optical methods are the best choice. Thus, graphene-derivatives are widely introduced in this field, regarding to the close contact of optical sensors with fluorescent emissions. Several methods for Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ detection are listed in
Tables 1–3, respectively. According to Table 1, despite high sensitivity (≤ 1 ppm), fluorescent sensing method requires high-tech fluorometry system and expert labor. However, colorimetric sensing is an on-side, immediate, and simple form of sensing that can be detected through naked eye, even by un-experts [44].

Linearity should be affirmed for the intended working range, incorporating the chosen matrix. A linear range can be calculated from the linearity evaluation experiments, though, the criteria for this calculation can be different. Regarding to ion detection, the signal intensities span that present a linear relationship between the concentration of the analyte ion, and intensity of the signal recorded by the sensing system. In this experimental study, the LLOQ for Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ detection was estimated 0.1 ppm.

As a thin, packed layer of hexagonal carbon atoms, graphene is composed of six honeycomb lattice shape carbons with special features in terms of electricity and heat conduction, as well as mechanically strength. These properties make graphene derivatives potential materials to be used as biosensors [66]. However, a bare graphene has a poor dispensability in solvents and poor aggregation capability. This fact reveals that graphene can be altered to various forms such as quantum dots, nanoplatelets, nanoribbons, and colloids [67]. Moreover, the zero-band-gap semiconductor feature of graphene causes to the impossibility of the luminescence observation, resisting the direct usage of graphene in luminescence applications. However, the band gap of graphene can be controlled by manifesting the distribution, shape, and size of graphene nanostructures [68]. Quantum dots (QDs) are the answer which have the size varying from 1 to10 nm that has found broad attraction in sensor studies. QDs show quantum phenomena that provide remarkable benefits in optical characters, owing to their size. Evident is the fact that on excitation, the energy and intensity of emitted light is superior when the QDs dimensions becomes tinier [69]. QDs can be synthesized from carbon-based materials, metals, or semiconductors. With this fact, several quantum dots can be synthesized such as cadmium sulfide quantum dots, CdS quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, and GQDs [70]. In comparison with the other types of QDs, GQDs are more favorable for sensitive analytical sensor usages with various features such as high solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity for biological applications [71]. GQDs can be obtained by two types of methods, ‘top-down’ or bottom-up’. Regardless to the type of fabrication, in order to obtain graphene-based fluorescent materials, it is obligatory to convert 2D graphene sheets to 0D GQDs. This material possesses extraordinary characters due to the edge and quantum confinement. Both preparation methods have their specific advantages and disadvantages [72]. The ‘top-down’ method is easier than the other one, though, there is a lake control of physical features, size, and morphology of GQDs. Thus, through the ‘top-down’ approach, the band gap, electron transfer ability, and photoluminescence of GQDs can be controlled [73]. Respecting to the outstanding mechanical, optical, thermal, and electrical properties of GQDs, it has known as one of the most attractive choices to be used as sensors to recognize toxic metal ions. Regarding to the close contacts of optical sensors with fluorescent emissions, GQD has been considered as an excellent choice [74]. With this aim we used citric acid to synthesize GQD with adjusted band gap, which has three carboxyl groups on its edge. Moreover, pertaining to raise the intensity of the fluorometry peak of GQD, it is proposed to conjugate a material with electron-rich functional groups to GQD [75]. With this aim, in this research study, we functionalize GQD particles with DPA which has amine and carboxyl groups on its structure. This drug is known as a chelator for copper ions to heal WD patients [76,77]. The copper ion can be chelated through binding with the thiol and amine groups of DPA [78,79]. Therefore, we synthesized GQD-DPA in this study to recognize GQD-DPA through both colorimetric, and fluorometry methods.

The XRD pattern of GQD (Fig. S2) reveals a broad peak located at around 25θ (0.36 nm), indicating that the electrochemical procedure has more active sites on the facial parts of GQD. Moreover, it is indicated that, in comparison with graphene, the peak intensity raised to higher degrees in GQD that can be attributed to the carbonization of citric acid regarding to produce graphite structures with more compact interlayer spacing than the original graphene.

To further confirm the deprotonation and protonation of the DPA terminal thiol groups in contact with GQD, as well as the terminal amine functional groups in contact with the amino acids and different ions, the zeta potential, which associates with the local environment and the surface charge of molecules, was examined. The zeta potential measurement is a useful approach with the ability to offer data on the net charge of nanoparticles and has an overview onto their stability. Fig. S2 (see Supporting information) indicates that the final size of the GQD-DPA reached to somewhere around 0.8 nm. Moreover, with these interactions between the facial functional groups of the GQD-DPA particles, it can be predicted that the total charge of the system might be turned to positive charge, because the electro-rich functional groups are reached to a stable state when interacting with each other. This fact is also illustrated in Fig. S2 (see Supporting information) which shows the total charge of the system around 10.5 mV.

In the presence of Cu2+, a considerable size increment was revealed for GQD-DPA particles, since the chelator behavior of DPA towards Cu2+. In fact, when Cu2+ is introducing to the system, the thiol groups of DPA might lose their interactions with the carboxyl groups of GQD, and participate in the new binds with Cu2+ through S−-Cu2+-S−, and NH−-Cu2+-NH−. Despite this molecular separation, the DLS results of Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA particles shows an increased profile in their size, in comparison with the bare GQD-DPA particles. Thus, it can be assumed that the terminal COO− groups on the DPA-Cu2+ rings are interacted with the surrounded amine groups of the adjacent GQD-DPA molecules, leading to further accumulation of GQD-DPA, and raising its dimension to 1.38 nm. However, in this process, since some of the DPA molecules are separated from GQD molecules and participated in the chelating reactions with Cu2+, the total electron density of the system is raised because of the presence of the electron-rich free carboxyl groups on the GQD, which has detached from DPA molecules in the presence Cu2+, reaching from 10.5 mV to 1.85 mV (Fig. S3 (see Supporting information)).

Likewise, in the presence of Fe3+ with three (H+), there is the possibility for triple binds of metal ion-amine group interactions. Since Fe3+ might be interacted with three DPA-GQD molecules through thiol and/or amine binds, the degree of GQD-DPA accumulation was further enhanced, even more than that in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. S4 (see Supporting information)). However, unlike Cu2+, since no GQD-DPA detachments was occurred in the presence of Fe3+, the total charge of the system is significantly shifted towards the positive charge (47 mV), because numerous electron-rich functional groups are interacted with Fe3+, and reached to a stable state (Fig. S4 (see Supporting information)).

In the presence of the other ions, GQD-DPA also showed accumulated profile because of the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged ions and the negatively-charged functional groups on GQD-DPA. Moreover, there is the possibility for the metal cations to interact with amine groups on GQD-DPA via NH−-metal ionn+-NH− binds. As shown in Fig. S5 (see Supporting information) the size of the GQD-DPA raised from 0.8 nm (for the bare GQD-DPA) to 991.4 nm (for GQD-DPA in the presence of metal cations), highlighting the accumulated profile of GQD-DPA in the presence of Pt4+. Moreover, Fig. S5 (see Supporting information) shows that the zeta potential of the GQD-DPA in the presence of Pt4+ is approximately similar to that for the bare GQD-DPA, with a slight decrease, reaching from 10.5 mV to 9.16 mV. This negligible decrement describes the electrostatic interactions between the positively-charged metal ion and the negatively-charged functional groups. NH−-metal ionn+-NH− binds also caused to slight decrease in the electron density of the system that is in accordance with the zeta potential results of Pt4+-interacted GQD-DPA.

In the presence of cysteine, a thiol-contained amino acid, the thiol groups of cysteine might interact with the carboxyl group of DPA, resulting to GQD-DPA accumulation. This accumulation is affirmed by DLS results which show the increment in the size of GQD-DPA particles supported cysteine from 0.8 nm (for the bare GQD-DPA) to 1.7 nm (for the GQD-DPA supported cysteine) (Fig. S6 (see Supporting information)).

It is predictable that in the presence of Fe3+, cysteine and some of the DPA molecules might leave GQD particles intimacies, regarding to form S−-Fe3+-S−, and NH−-Fe3+-NH− binds. This size decrement was confirmed by DLS results (Fig. S7 (see Supporting information)). According to the DLS results, the a decrement in the size of the GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Fe3+, from 1.7 nm to 0.6 nm, can be attributed to the contribution of three protons with free electrons of the thiol/amine functional groups of cysteine/DPA, which is accompanied by a size decrement for GQD-DPA particles. Evident is the fact that the contribution of the aforementioned functional groups in metal ion interactions caused to reduce the total amounts of free electrons from the system. This reduction can be affirmed by the zeta potential results which shows slight shifting in the charge of the system from 1.64 mV (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine) to 5.49 mV (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Fe3+) (Fig. S7 (see Supporting information)).

Fig. S8 (see Supporting information) illustrate the TEM image of GQD, which shows the well-distributed GQD particles. As previously mentioned, the distribution behavior of the graphene sheets can be manipulated during the formation of a zero-band-gap semiconductor, leading to provide a well-dispersed GQD solution.

Fig. S9 (see Supporting information) shows the AFM results of GQD-DPA (Fig. S9A), and GQD-DPA in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. S9B). It can be illustrated that the size of the GQD-DPA particles is increased after interacting with Cu2+, from 162 nm to 351 nm. This size increment might be attributed to the chelation of Cu2+ by DPA, resulting to GQD-DPA accumulation.

FT-IR spectrum of DPA-GQDs reveals a peak at 3434 cm−1 relate to the symmetric stretching vibration of amine group (NH2). The peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1691 cm−1 could be respectively belong to the CH and CO stretching vibration while 1766 and 1716 cm−1 peaks are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO in GQDs FT-IR spectra. The CO stretching vibrations downshift may be related to the H-bonding’s creation between DPA and GQDs (Fig. S10) [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-DPA sensing probe and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Regularly, the preparation of functionalized GQD follows a two-step process [80]. Firstly, GQD was obtained through a thermal pyrolysis process of organic acid or slicing graphene oxide via violently chemical oxidation. Secondly, specific functional groups were conjugated into the edge parts of GQD by covalent interactions between the active species on the GQD and the functional groups of the reagent. Since GQD possesses large amounts of electrons on its graphene sheet, the CO covalent bonds in the system are substantially weakened when the p-π conjugations are created between the graphene sheets and the marginal oxygen atoms. Thus, this procedure leads GQD to lose its facial active groups within the functionalization process [32]. This will further lead to a relatively poor structural stability and low rate of GQD functionalization. Moreover, the functionalization procedure may cause to a substantial decrement in the fluorescence intensity because of partially loss of the active species [33]. Regarding to solve the above issue, a novel strategy was proposed for GQD-DPA fabrication, which follows a single-step preparation method. This preparation step is based on the thermal pyrolysis of DPA and citric acid that are using as the carbon sources for GQD-DPA synthesis. The fabrication and functionalization of graphene sheets were simultaneously occurred through the thermal pyrolysis.

Based on the relevant chemical features and structural properties of DPA and citric acid, it was indicated that the critic acid has significant role in guaranteeing the generation of graphene sheets via the carbonization and dehydration, while DPA is principally applied to introduce the side groups into the GQD edges to raise its optical properties via the covalent interplays between DPA and citric acid [32]. Owing to great reactive activity, the interaction between DPA and citric acid may leads to the generation of stable chemical linkages. The characteristic supports the conformational stability of DPA-GQD. In this system, the ratio of citric acid and DPA (CA/DPA) is a pivotal factor for effecting on the functionalization degree and creation of graphene sheets, which causing to diverse optical features. Generally, it is believed that more ratio of citric acid is essential for the generation of bigger graphene sheets, though, lower levels of functionalization is occurred in this scenario. On the other hand, the amount of DPA is a determinative parameter to enhance the functionalization degree, but smaller graphene sheets are appeared in this case. This is due to the consumption of a great amount of active groups on the citric acid after reacting with DPA. This provides the ability to decrease the graphene sheets through a pyrolysis process within citric acid molecules, causing to create smaller graphene sheets with DPA increment in the system. However, graphene quantum dots cannot be obtained if the proportion of DPA/CA exceeds a determined value. Regarding to evaluate the impact of DPA/CA on the characteristics of the final product, researchers synthesized DPA-GQDs with various ratios DPA/CA. They concluded that the good functionalization and high fluorescence were obtained when the mass ratio of DPA/CA is 0.4 [32]. Therefore we used the same ratio of DPA/CA in our study to prepare GQD-DPA. As shown in Fig. S11 (see Supporting information), the fluorescence intensity can be significantly increase after introducing DPA molecules with free electron-rich functional groups. This fact is related to the electron-rich hydroxyl and amino groups of DPA-GQD on the edge parts of the graphene sheets. It can be assumed that in the presence of DPA, the thiol groups might fill the gaps and defects in the gap bind of GQD, leading to raise the fluorometry peak intensity (Fig. S11 (see Supporting information)).

Moreover, the electron donating moieties of DPA on the GQDs molecules cause to enhance the density of electrons on the graphene sheets through the electron p-π conjugations, leading to an increased fluorescence emission and excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Cu2+ by GQD-DPA sensing solution
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 1, the color of the synthesized GQD-DPA solution was altered from light brown to dark brown in the presence of Cu2+, though, the presence of the other ions did not show any significant effects on the color of the sensing solution. Therefore, Cu2+ can be selectively detected by the naked eye through color alternation.

As previously mentioned, according to Scheme 1A, in the presence of DPA, there is the possibility for the facial free carboxyl groups on GQD to interact with the thiol groups of the DPA. Thus, GQD-DPA might have free amine and carboxyl groups on their structure. It is known that DPA acts as a chelator for Cu2+ [81–83]. Generally speaking, chelating factors are inorganic or organic compounds that can make binds with metal ions to create complex ring-like constructs called “chelates”. The processes of these chelation are involved either one covalent or two covalent linkages and two co-ordinates or one coordinate binds in terms of bi-dentate chelates. Regarding to this fact, atoms like O, S, and N act as ligand atoms in the sort of chemical groups such as OPO3H, S-S, SH, NH, NH2, OH, and > CO. Multidentate or bidentate ligands create ring structures which contained the metal ion moiety and the two-ligand atoms coupled to the metal. Many donors play role as bi-dentate ligands. Five-element chelate ring structures are typically stable and they are created by ligands with the form of YCCY skeletons like Y-CO-CH2-Y, Y-CH2-CH2-Y, etc. where Y is NR, O, S, OR, NR2, and etc. Moreover, there are further instances of inorganic chelate ligands that form five-element rings with metal ions. In the most elementary example, a proton (H+) which is capable to attract the single pair of electrons of the chelator might be engaged in the formation of a coordination complex. Metal cations such as Cu2+, and the transition metal ions such as Fe3+ can be involved in this type of complex formation [84]. Therefore, considering DPA as Cu2+ chelator, in our work, when Cu2+ is chelated by DPA, the interactions between the thiol groups of DPA might be detached from GQD structure and form new bonds with Cu2+. This causes to partial removal of the DPA-related functional groups (amine and carboxyl groups with free electrons) from GQD molecules. Thus the electrical charge of the system changed from 10.5 mV (for the bare GQD-DPA) to 1.85 mV (for the Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA molecules). Moreover, since some of the DPA molecules are still attached to the GQD molecules, the free COO− groups on the Cu2+-chelated DPA complexes possess the capability to interact with the free amine groups of the other intact GQD-DPA, leading to form GQD-DPA clusters in the presence of Cu2+. To confirm this size increment of GQD-DPA in the presence of Cu2+ the DLS analysis was performed that indicated the size increment from 0.8 nm for the bare GQD-DPA to 1.38 nm for GQD-DPA chelated Cu2+ complex. This fact is the main reason for the color alternation of GQD-DPA in the presence of Cu2+ from light orange to dark orange ( Fig. 2). The same procedures are occurred in the presence of Hg2+, in accordance with the previous studies that introduced DPA as a sensing agent for mercury ion [85,86]. In fact, high thiophilicity of Hg2+ leads to detach the thiol-carboxyl group binds of DPA-GQD, and participate DPA in the new bonds of thiolHg2+. Thus, GQD-DPA might be accumulated in the presence of Hg2+ which is accompanied by the GQD-DPA color alternation in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 2).

As shown in Fig. 2, apart from Cu2+, Pt4+, and Fe3+, the presence of the other ions had not shown any significant effects on the color of the GQD-DPA. However, the intensity of their fluorometry peak showed slight decrease (please refer to the next section for in-depth description). This slight decrement in the fluorometry peak intensity is related to the interactions of the metal ions with the free amine groups of the GQD-DPA through ‘N-ionn+-N’ binds. These metal ion-QGD-DPA interactions cause to a slight decrement in the amounts of free electrons on the GQD-DPA, regarding to metal ion-amine groups interactions. Moreover, the negatively-charged functional groups on GQD-DPA might be attracted to the positively-charged metal cations and form metal ion-GQD-DPA complexes via electrostatic binds. This fact is confirmed by the comparison of the zeta potential results of Pt4+-interacted-GQD-DPA with the bare GQD-DPA. According to obtained results by DLS/zeta potential, the zeta potential of GQD-DPA decreases from 10.5 mV to 9.16 mV in the presence of Pt4+, highlighting the fact of Hg2+ interactions with the facial amine groups of GQD-DPA through ‘NHg2+ N’ bonds. In addition, ‘Nmetal ionN’ bonds lead to partial GQD-DPA accumulation in the presence of metal ions (Scheme 1A). However, this accumulation is lesser than that for the Cu2+-chelated-GQD-DPA which causes to no color alternations. According to the obtained results, the dimension of Pt4+-interacted-GQD-DPA, which indicated that the size of the bare GQD-DPA is increased from 0.8 nm to 991.4 nm in the presence of Pt4+, though, this size is still smaller than the Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA molecule dimensions (1.38 nm). Besides Cu2+ metal cation, as mentioned before, Fe3+ is a transition metal ion that is also capable to be involved in a coordination complex through Fe3+-thiol and/or Fe3+-amine interactions. As previously mentioned, multidentate or bidentate ligands create ring structures which contained the metal ion moiety and the two-ligand atoms coupled to the metal. Thus, Fe3+ can be linked with three DPA molecules by interacting with the thiol and/or amine groups of GQD-DPA. It can be predictable that in the presence of Fe3+, the GQD-DPA molecule has higher dimension in comparison with that in the presence of Cu2+, owing to more degree of GQD-DPA molecule accumulation as the result of the triple thiol/amine bindings in the presence of Fe3+ ion, owing to the presence of three positive charges. This claim can be confirmed by measuring the particle size of Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA by DLS analysis, which is estimated around 3.5 nm. It is obvious that the dimension of Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA is far more than the dimension of Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA (1.38 nm). This significant increment in the dimension of GQD-DPA in the presence of Fe3+ can be observed through color alternation of GQD-DPA from light orange (in the bare state) to dark orange (in the presence of Fe3+) (Fig. 2). This fact can be confirmed by measuring the zeta potential of Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA and compare with the bare state of GQD-DPA. According to the obtained data, results of zeta potential shows significant reduction of electron density on the GQD-DPA after adding Fe3+, reaching from 10.5 mV (for bare GQD-DPA) to 47 mV for Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA. This can highlight the fact that some of the electron-rich amine groups on the GQD-DPA surface are involved in Fe3+ interactions. Moreover, the comparison between the zeta potential results of Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA and Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA shows significant reduction in the electron density of the system in the presence of Fe3+, reaching the charge of the system from 1.85 mV (in the presence of Cu2+) to 47 mV (in the presence of Fe3+). This result also confirms the occupation of the electron-rich NH2 groups in Fe3+ interactions. According to Fig. 2, Hg2+ can also be detected by GQDs-DPA. Previously, researchers concluded that DPA can act as a chelator for the mercury ion. Regarding to the chelating attitude of DPA towards Cu2+, more concentrations of Cu2+ causing to trigger more DPA molecules, and in consequence, more GQD-DPA agglomeration. Thus, very low concentrations (≪ 0.1 ppm) cannot be detected by colorimetry tests, because of inadequate GQD-DPA agglomeration. Though, the concentration of ≥ 0.1 ppm can effectively detected by colorimetric assays (Fig. S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of Cu2+ by GQD-DPA sensing solution
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding to evaluate the fluorescence response of GQD-DPA solution towards Cu2+ detection, the fluorescence intensity of GQD-DPA was recorded in the absence and presence of Cu2+ along with Al3+, As3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+ ( Fig. 3). It is obvious that the addition of Cu2+ leads to a significant decrement in the fluorescence intensity of GQD-DPA. It can be assumed that in the presence of DPA, the thiol groups might remove the gaps and defects in the gap bind of GQD, leading to raise the fluorometry peak intensity of GQDs. In the presence of metal cations, in particular the ions with high thiophilicity (such as Hg2+, and Fe3+), and ions with chelating capability by DPA (such as Cu2+), the thiol binds of the DPA might be detached from GQD, regarding to form the new binds with the metal ions. These interactions might lead to reduce the fluorometry peak intensity of GQD-DPA in the presence of the mentioned ions, owing to the fact that the defects might be regenerated in the gap bind of GQD, after removing the thiol groups of DPA.

As shown in Scheme 1A, in the presence of DPA, free carboxyl groups on GQD interact with the thiol groups of the DPA. Thus, GQD-DPA might have free electron-rich groups on their constructs. It is known that DPA acts as a chelator for Cu2+ [81–83]. When Cu2+ is chelated by DPA, the thiol groups might be detached from GQD and interact with Cu2+, causing to partial removal of amine and carboxyl groups, with free electrons, from GQD molecules, and therefore, decrease the intensity of the fluorometry peak. Moreover, since some of the DPA molecules have still linked to the GQD molecules, the free COO− groups on the Cu2+-chelated DPA complexes possess the capability to interact with the free amine groups of the other intact GQD-DPA, leading to form GQD-DPA clusters in the presence of Cu2+. This clustering procedure provides bigger GQD-DPA strains with smaller band gap, causing to decrease the electron excitation and emission energy. Thus, besides the lower electron-rich functional groups on the surface of the GQD molecules, their bigger dimensions in the presence of Cu2+ ions cause to quench the fluorometry peak intensity in comparison with the bare GQD-DPA. Despite the quenching behavior of the GQD-DPA fluorometry peak in the presence of Cu2+, the intensity of this peak is still more than that for the bare GQD, indicating that some DPA molecules are still remained intact on GQD molecules, regardless their accumulated profile. Hence, it is obvious that more concentrations of Cu2+ causes to more DPA-Cu2+ interactions, and in consequence, more functional groups may be removed from the GQD molecules and participate in Cu2+ chelation, leading to more fluorescence quenching behavior in the presence of higher Cu2+ ion concentrations. The same procedures are occurred in the presence of Hg 2+, in accordance with the previous studies that introduced DPA as a sensing agent for mercury ion [85,86]. In fact, high thiophilicity of Hg2+ leads to detach the thiol-carboxyl group binds of DPA-GQD, and participate DPA in the new bonds of thiolHg2+. Thus, GQD-DPA might be accumulated in the presence of Hg2+, and its relevant fluorometry peak intensity might be quenched (Fig. 3).

However, the quenching amount of the Hg2+-interacted-GQD-DPA fluorometry peak is still lower in than that for the Cu2+-chelated-GQD-DPA. This fact can be attributed to the chelating procedure of Cu2+ which causes to create DPA rings, and thus, more GQD-DPA agglomeration might be occurred in the presence of Cu2+. The fluorometry peaks of GQD-DPA in the presence of several metal ions are occurred in the lower levels than that for the bare GQD-DPA. This slight decrement is related to the interactions of the metal ions with the free amine groups of the GQD-DPA through ‘N-ionn+-N’ binds, which cause to a slight decrement in the amounts of free electrons on the GQD-DPA, due to the metal ion-amine groups interactions. However, this accumulation is lesser than that for the Cu2+-chelated-GQD-DPA. Therefore, the fluorometry peak intensity of the Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA molecule is appeared in a considerable lesser level than them for both the GQD-DPA, and Pt4+-GQD-DPA molecules.

In the presence of Fe3+, the GQD-DPA molecule are more accumulated in comparison with that in the presence of Cu2+, owing to more degree of H+ on Fe3+. This claim can be confirmed by measuring the particle size of Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA by DLS analysis, which is incredibly increased than the bare GQD-DPA, and even Cu2+-chelated-GQD-DPA. However, despite the fact that the accumulated GQD-DPA resulted to lower fluorometric peak intensity, the fluorometry spectra of GQD-DPA show more intense peak in the presence of Fe3+ than in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 3). This antithesis may be assumed to the higher electron density on GQD-DPA molecules in the presence of Cu2+, than that in the presence Fe3+. This lower degree of electron density for Fe3+-interacted-GQD-DPA molecules is related to the extra proton portion on Fe3+ which causes to employ extra amine groups, leading to the presence of lower electron-rich functional groups on the Fe3+-interacted GQD-DPA molecules than that of Cu2+-chelated GQD-DPA molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies of ion detection by GQD-DPA in biological sample
Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ in urine sample by GQD-DPA
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding to detect Cu2+ in urine samples, GQD-DPA was applied. Fig. S19 (see Supporting information) shows the color alternation of GQD-DPA in the presence of Cu2+ from light brown to dark brown, while this color alternation was not obvious in the presence of the other applied ions. However, Fe3+ and Pt4+ can also affect the color of GQD-DPA, but their color alternation is lighter than that in the presence of Cu2+. The matter of the fact is that since DPA is the chelator for Cu2+, the thiol-carboxyl binds between the DPA and GQD might be ruined in the presence of Cu2+, and new binds might be formed between Cu2+ and DPA through the thiol and amine groups of DPA. This DPA ring has COO− on its edges, leading to make extra binds with the surrounded GQD-DPA. This interactions between the DPA rings and the surrounded GQD-DPA might cause GQD molecule accumulation, resulting to color alternation of the GQD-DPA solution in the presence of Cu2+. Moreover, in the presence of Fe3+, since this ion has a high tendency to interact with the thiol/amine groups of DPA, some interactions might be occurred with Fe3+, and GQD-DPA particles agglomeration might be occurred which is accompanied by the color alternation of the sensing solution. In the presence of Pt4+, it can be predicted that this ion might make interactions with the surrounded electron-rich functional groups through electrostatic attractions, leading to GQD-DPA molecules accumulation, and in consequence, color alternation of GQD-DPA solution. All of the aforementioned colorimetric results are in confirmation with the fluorometry and DLS results, as discussed in the previous sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometry detection of Cu2+ in urine sample by GQD-DPA
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the last section, the presence of Cu2+ causing DPA to make a ring to chelate Cu2+, and leading to interact with the surrounded GQD-DPA through the interactions between COO and the surrounded free amine groups of GQD-DPA. This phenomena is accompanied by the GQD-DPA agglomeration. This agglomeration causing to quench the fluorometry peak of the GQD-DPA in urine sample, as shown in Fig. S20 (see Supporting information). Besides, the contribution of the electron-rich amine groups on the edges of GQD-DPA molecules in the COO− interactions leading to reduce the total free electron density of the system, and in consequence, reduce the intensity of the GQD-DPA fluorometry peak. Likewise, the interactions between Fe3+ with the surrounded GQD-DPA through thiol/amine groups, besides electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged amine groups and the metal cation, leading to GQD-DPA accumulation and fluorometry peak quenching. In this case, free electrons of the system might be involved in the ionic interactions, leading to quench the fluorometry peak. According to Fig. S20 (see Supporting information), it is also obvious that the intensity of the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA is decreased in the presence of Hg2+, in which, the thiol binds of DPA might be distracted in the presence of Hg2+, owing to the high thiophilicity of Hg2+, and form the new binds of S-Hg2+-S in urine sample. Thus, the fluorometry peak of the sensing solution is quenched. However, the GQD-DPA peak intensity in the presence of Cu2+ is still lower than that in the presence of Hg2+, highlighting the chelating behavior of DPA towards Cu2+.

More concentrations of Cu2+ ions causing to trigger more DPA molecules in the chelation process, therefore, more GQD-DPA accumulation might be occurred. With this reason, more concentration of Cu2+ causing to more GQD-DPA fluorometry peak quenching in urine samples (Fig. S21 (see Supporting information)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Fe3+, and Pt4+ by GQD-DPA, using μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 reveals the optical micrographs for the Fe3+, and Pt4+ detection assays performed on μPADs. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the optical images on the μPAD was captured under natural light, in which the color alternations of the GQD-DPA (Fig. 4) were evaluated through the μ-channels, via capillary method. The reason might be due to the disruption of the GQD-DPA aggregation when interfering with Fe3+, and Pt4+ ions, as described in the previous sections. In the presence of GQD-DPA, after 15 min, it was revealed that Fe3+ was appeared in orange color on the paper, while Cu2+, and Hg2+ are not apparently detected on the paper. According to the DLS results, GQD-DPA in the presence of Fe3+ showed higher dimensions (3.5 nm), than in the presence of Cu2+ (1.38 nm). Thus, the accumulated GQD-DPA particles might be maintained on the paper, while the smaller particles are passed through the paper, leading to Fe3+ detection on the μPAD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ by GQD-DPA supported amino acids sensing solution
Colorimetric detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In the second part, we evaluated the role of amino acids in combination with GQD-DPA to recognize different ions. As shown in Fig. 5, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions can be detected by GQD-DPA either in the bare state, or in the amino acids-supported states with the concentration of 10 mM. The fact that Cu2+, and Hg2+ can be detected in the presence of all of the amino acids in GQD-DPA sensing solution, regardless to the type of amino acids, highlights the chelating, and sensing role of DPA to detect Cu2+, and Hg2+, respectively.

When the concentrations of amino acids were reached to the higher levels (from 10 mM to 1 M), the colorimetric behavior of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ was altered. As shown in Fig. 6, the color of the GQD-DPA supported by cysteine amino acid was changed from light brown to blue in the presence of Fe3+ ion. In addition, similar to that in the lower concentrations (10 mM), cysteine can also detect Hg2+ in higher concentrations (1 M).

The matter of the fact is that in the presence of cysteine, the free carboxyl groups on the GQD-DPA molecules may react with the free thiol groups of cysteine, causing to raise the dimension of GQD-DPA particles. This size increment in the presence of cysteine was confirmed through DLS results, which indicated that the size of the particles increases from 0.8 nm (for bare GQD-DPA) to 1.7 (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M)). Moreover, the surface electron density of the system was increased after adding cysteine, which may be attributed to the introduction of the electron-rich carboxyl and amine groups of the cysteine to the system. This charge decrement is revealed in the zeta potential results which was reduced from 10.5 mV (for the bare GQD-DPA) to 1.6 mV (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine). As mentioned before, in the presence of Fe3+, a transition metal ion, DPA might act as a detector for this ion by forming S-Fe3+, and NH--Fe3+ binds. The same reactions might also be occurred in the cysteine-supported GQD-DPA, where thiol moieties are existed. Likewise, owing to the high thiophilicity of Hg2+, the thiolcarboxyl bonds between the cysteine and GQD-DPA molecules and/or GQD and DPA molecules might be broken in the presence of Hg2+ to form new S-Hg2+-S binds. These results are affirmed by the DLS and zeta potential data, which indicate that the size of the cysteine-supported GQD-DPA was decreased in reaction with Fe3+, from 1.7 to 0.64 nm, and the total electron density of the system might have slight reduction, altering the total charge from 1.64 mV (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine) to 5.49 mV (for GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Fe3+). Apart from cysteine, the addition of other amino acids (methionine, alanine, arginine, valine, and proline), with no thiol groups, showed the quenching behavior in the presence of both Cu2+, and Hg2+ ions. The in-depth evaluation of the amino acid-based ion recognition was testified through fluorometry and colorimetric results. After adding Cu2+, DPA might acts as chelator and reacts with Cu2+ through NH−-Cu2+-NH−, and S−-Cu2+-S− binds, and forming new clusters. Furthermore, in the presence of Hg2+, respecting to the high thiophilicity of this ion, the thiol-carboxyl binds of GQD-DPA might be detached and contributed in the new binds of S-Hg2+-S. Thus, similar to Cu2+, new clusters might be formed in the presence of Hg2+. Fig. 6, also shows the colorimetric behavior of GQD-DPA supported arginine in the presence of various ions. The colorimetric profile of GQD-DPA supported arginine shows that the color of the sensing solution was altered from light brown to blue in the presence of Cu2+. This indication also is in accordance with the aforementioned descriptions about the Cu2+ chelating procedure in the GQD-DPA-based sensing solution. Considering GQD-DPA supported proline, the colorimetric evaluation showed color alternation of the GQD-DPA supported proline from light brown to light green, after adding Cu2+, and to white after adding Hg2+. Cu2+-proline contains chelation between the carboxylate group and two oxygen in a zwitterion proline. Therefore, the GQD-DPA supported proline followed a quenching trend in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ by GQD-DPA supported amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The role of amino acids in combination with GQD-DPA was evaluated to recognize different ions. The fact that Cu2+, and Hg2+ can be recognized in the presence of all of the amino acids supported GQD-DPA sensing solution, regardless to the type of amino acids, highlights the chelating, and sensing ability of DPA to detect Cu2+, and Hg2+, respectively. In the presence of cysteine, the free carboxyl groups on the GQD-DPA molecules may react with the free thiol groups of cysteine, leading to GQD-DPA particle size increment. This size increment in the presence of cysteine was confirmed through DLS results. Thus, the intensity of the fluorometry peak of the GQD-DPA was significantly decreased after adding cysteine to the solution, confirming the size increment of GQD-DPA supported cysteine molecules. It can be assumed that the presence of cysteine caused to create defects (extra bind) in the bind gap of GQD-DPA, thus, leading to quench the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA. After adding ions with high thiophilicity, such as Hg2+ and Fe3+, or ions with chelating ability such as Cu2+, the thiol groups of cysteine might detached from the carboxyl groups of DPA. This interactions might remove the defects in the gap bind of GQD-DPA and cause to raise the intensity of the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ (Fig. S13 (see Supporting information)).
The fluorometry spectra of GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) in the presence of different ions, standard solution (1 µg/ml) (A), peak intensity versus type of ion (B) (number of repeat = 3).

Similar to GQD-DPA supported cysteine, the fluorometry spectra of GQD-DPA supported methionine was also follow the same trend as cysteine (Fig. S14 (see Supporting information)). The reason might be attributed to the presence of S group in the chemical structure of methionine, which can be involved in the chelation procedure of Cu2+. These S groups of methionine cause to create defects (extra bind) in the gap bind of GQD-DPA, leading to quench the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA in the presence of methionine. After Cu2+ addition to the system, S binds of methionine might be detached from carboxyl groups of DPA, regarding to form S-Cu2+-S interactions. Thus, the defects (extra binds) might be removed from the gap bind of GQD-DPA, and causing to raise the intensity of the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA supported methionine in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. S14 (see Supporting information)).

Moreover, the surface electron density of the system was increased after adding cysteine, which may be attributed to the introduction of the electron-rich carboxyl and amine groups of the cysteine to the system. When cysteine is added to the system, the interactions between the thiol groups of the cysteine and the free carboxyl groups on the edges of the GQD-DPA might cause to GQD-DPA size increment in the presence of cysteine, leading to significant quench in the GQD-DPA fluorometry peak. Regarding to the fact that cysteine is a thiol-contained amino acid and possesses OH, and SH groups which are the chelating sites, this amino acid can act as a chelating agent for metal cations such as Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+. Therefore, in the presence of these ions, the thiol-carboxyl binds of cysteine-DPA might be distracted to form new chelating binds between the cysteine molecules and the specific metal cations (Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+). Consequently, the agglomeration between the GQD-DPA particles might be distracted, leading to raise the fluorometry peak intensity in the presence of the mentioned ions. Moreover, since the electron-rich amine groups on GQD-DPA are losing their binds with thiol groups during the mentioned bind destruction process, in the presence of Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ ions the density of the free electrons might be raised, which is also another reason for raising the fluorometry peak intensity. However, Cu2+ is in the influence of two chelating agents (cysteine, and DPA), thus, the fluorometry peak intensity of GQD-DPA supported cysteine is lower in the presence of Cu2+, than that for Fe3+, and Hg2+. These results are affirmed by the DLS and zeta potential data, which indicate that the size of the cysteine-supported GQD-DPA was remarkably increased in reaction with Fe3+, and the total electron density of the system might have slight reduction. According to the fluorometry spectra of the GQD-DPA supported amino acids, the significant increment in the peak intensity is obvious for all of the amino acids. This indicates forming new binds between the amine groups of the amino acids with free carboxyl groups of the GQD-DPA, leading to reduce the total electron density of the system. Thus, the quenching behavior of the fluorometry peak might not only be attributed to the further accumulation of GQD-DPA, but also to the occupation of the electron-rich functional groups in the amino acids interactions. As shown in Figs. S15 and S16 (see Supporting information), the intensities of the peaks related to GQD-DPA supported alanine and GQD-DPA supported valine were obviously quenched in the presence of both Cu2+, and Hg2+. Since these amino acids are composed of a carboxyl group and an amine group, it can be assumed that in the presence of alanine and valine, the reactions between the amine/carboxyl groups of these amino acids with the free amine/carboxyl groups of GQD-DPA might be occurred, leading to a significant reduction in the peak intensity of the GQD-DPA, owing to the stability of the electron-rich functional groups, as mentioned before. After adding Cu2+, DPA might acts as chelator and reacts with Cu2+ through N-Cu2+-N, and S-Cu2+-S binds, and forming new clusters, leading to decrease the fluorometry peaks intensities in both cases. Furthermore, in the presence of Hg2+, respecting to the high thiophilicity of this ion, the thiol-carboxyl binds of GQD-DPA might be detached and contributed in the new binds of S-Hg2+-S. Thus, similar to Cu2+, new clusters might be formed in the presence of Hg2+ and decrease the intensity of the fluorometry peaks (Fig. S15 (see Supporting information)), and (Fig. S16 (see Supporting information)). Fig. S17 (see Supporting information) shows the fluorometric behavior of GQD-DPA supported arginine in the presence of various ions. Moreover, the quenching attitude of the GQD-DPA supported arginine in the presence of Cu2+, and Hg2+ reaffirm the accumulation of the GQD molecules, as well as the consumption of the electron-rich moieties in the new binds. The similar procedure might be occurred in GQD-DPA supported proline, in which, the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA is quenched in the presence of Cu2+, and Hg2+, as the result of GQD-DPA accumulation, and the free electron density decrement (Fig. S18 (see Supporting information)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ by GQD-DPA, and GQD-DPA supported amino acids sensing solution in biologic sample
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the need of body to copper to support develop tissues that make up ligaments, joints, and bones, if the dosage of this metal ion exceeds a certain level (140 µg/dL (1.4 ppm)), it generate toxic effects in body. Thus, it is critically important to design a biosensor to recognize the Cu2+ in the low concentrations, regarding to early detection of the copper-related diseases, such as Wilson’s disease, hepatitis, liver disease, anemia, thyroid issues, rheumatoid arthritis, lymphoma, and leukemia. In this work, we fabricated a sensitive and selective biosensor to recognize Cu2+ in urine samples in the low dosage of 0.1 ppm through rapid colorimetric assay that can simply be detected via naked eye. Since amino acids are intrinsically existed in humans’ body, it is imperative to provide a sensing assay which can detect Cu2+ even in the presence several amino acids. The proposed copper ion sensing approach in this work can also satisfy this issue by recognizing Cu2+ in the presence of valine, proline, methionine, alanine, arginine, and cysteine, via both colorimetric and fluorometry approaches. The following sections have an in-depth overview on these recognizing methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of ions in urine sample by GQD-DPA supported amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing behavior of GQD-DPA towards various metal cations was evaluated in the presence of several amino acids, with or without thiol groups, among glycine, proline, valine, Leucine, methionine, alanine, arginine, and cysteine. According to Fig. S22 (see Supporting information), Hg2+, and Cu2+ can be detected in GQD-DPA supported cysteine, and GQD-DPA supported arginine, both in urine samples. Regardless of the type of amino acids, all of amino acids have at least an amine group and a carboxyl group on their edges. These functional groups on the amino acids cause to raise the level of accumulation of GQD-DPA particles in the presence of all amino acids.

Apart from cysteine, in the presence of the other amino acids, Cu2+ causes to detach the thiol groups of DPA from the carboxyl groups of GQD, and contributed them in the Cu2+ chelating binds. This interactions are accompanied by GQD-DPA agglomeration, and electron density reduction of the system, which cause to reduce the fluorometry peak intensity. The same thiol-carboxyl group detaching procedure might be occurred in the presence of Hg2+, owing to the high thiophilicity of this ion, and causing to GQD-DPA agglomeration. This increment in agglomeration is the main reason of color alternation. In the presence of cysteine, the interactions between the thiol groups of the cysteine and the free carboxyl groups on the edges of the GQD-DPA might cause to GQD-DPA size increment in the presence of cysteine, leading to a significant quench in the GQD-DPA fluorometry peak. Regarding to the fact that cysteine is a thiol-contained amino acid and possesses OH, and SH groups which are the chelating sites, this amino acid can act as a chelating agent for metal cations such as Hg2+, and Cu2+. Therefore, in the presence of these ions, the thiol-carboxyl binds of cysteine-DPA might be distracted to form new chelating binds between the cysteine molecules and the specific metal cations (Hg2+ and Cu2+). Consequently, the agglomeration between the GQD-DPA particles might be distracted, leading to color alternation of the sensing solution. It can be obvious that the color of the cysteine-supported GQD-DPA solution is shifting towards the color of the bare GQD-DPA sensing solution, after Cu2+ introduction, highlighting the fact that lower amounts of DPA molecules are participated in the chelating procedure of Cu2+, because of the chelating role of cysteine (Fig. S22 (see Supporting information)).
Colorimetric detection of ions (1 µg/ml) using GQD-DPA supported cysteine and arginine (1 M) human urine sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometry detection of ions in urine sample by GQD-DPA supported amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, since all of amino acids have at least one amine group and one carboxyl group on their edges, the level of accumulation of GQD-DPA particles in the presence of all amino acids is lower than that for the bare GQD-DPA. Besides, the amino acid-DPA interactions cause to reduce the electron density of the system, leading to GQD-DPA fluorometry peak quenching (Fig. S23 (see Supporting information)).

When cysteine was added to the system, the interactions between the thiol groups of the cysteine and the free carboxyl groups on the edges of the GQD-DPA might cause to GQD-DPA size increment in the presence of cysteine, leading to significant quench in the GQD-DPA fluorometry peak. Regarding to the fact that cysteine is a thiol-contained amino acid and possesses OH, and SH groups which are the chelating sites, this amino acid can act as a chelating agent for metal cations such as Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+. Therefore, in the presence of these ions, the thiol-carboxyl binds of cysteine-DPA might be distracted to form new chelating binds between the cysteine molecules and the specific metal cations (Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+). Consequently, the agglomeration between the GQD-DPA particles might be distracted, leading to raise the fluorometry peak intensity in the presence of the mentioned ions. Moreover, since the electron-rich amine groups on GQD-DPA are losing their binds with thiol groups during the mentioned bind destruction process, in the presence of Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ ions the density of the free electrons might be raised, which is also another reason for raising the fluorometry peak intensity. However, Cu2+ is in the influence of two chelating agents (cysteine, and DPA), thus, the fluorometry peak intensity of GQD-DPA supported cysteine is lower in the presence of Cu2+, than that for Fe3+, and Hg2+ (Fig. S24 (see Supporting information)). It can be assumed that the presence of cysteine caused to create defects (extra bind) in the bind gap of GQD-DPA, thus, leading to quench the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA. After adding ions with high thiophilicity, such as Hg2+ and Fe3+, or ions with chelating ability such as Cu2+, the thiol groups of cysteine might detached from the carboxyl groups of DPA. This interactions might remove the defects in the gap bind of GQD-DPA and cause to raise the intensity of the fluorometry peak of GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ in human urine sample.

Fig. S25 (see Supporting information) shows the fluorometric behavior of GQD-DPA supported arginine in the presence of various ions in urine sample. Moreover, the quenching attitude of the GQD-DPA supported arginine in the presence of Cu2+, and Hg2+ reaffirm the accumulation of the GQD molecules, as well as the consumption of the electron-rich moieties in the new binds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of optimum type of additive for colorimetric identification of ions
ce:section.ce:para: As previously mentioned, heavy metal ions are extremely ubiquitous environmental pollutants which are hazardous for human health. Therefore, a sensitive and simple method for heavy metal ions detection in aqueous solutions is a necessity. In this research study, various amino acids were evaluated to reveal the colorimetric sensing of the Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ ions. The applied amino acids are among l-glycine, l-Leucine, l-alanine, l-arginine, l-aspartic acid, l-valine, l-cysteine, and l-methionine, all in low concentration (10 mM), and in high concentration of 1 M. With regard to GQD-DPA supported amino acids, according to the amino acids structure, amine/carboxyl groups of amino acids can interact with carboxyl/amine groups of GQD-DPA molecules by weak hydrogen linkages. The thiol-containing amino acids such as cysteine can specifically detect Hg2+ from the other ions by forming the stable complex of Hg (Cys)2 [87]. The of GQD-DPA supported cysteine in which two cysteine molecules are linked with each other through thiol-thiol groups, from one side, and linked with GQD-DPA molecules through weak hydrogen binds between the amine/carboxylic acid groups of cysteine and carboxylic acid/amine groups of GQD-DPA. In the presence of Hg2+, the thiol-Hg2+-thiol interactions caused to GQD-DPA supported cysteine (10 mM) agglomeration that is accompanied by color alternation of the GQD-DPA solution (Fig. 5). Moreover, in the presence of Cu2+, regardless the types of amino acids, the chelating binds between DPA and copper ion overcomes the weak hydrogen interactions between the amine/carboxylic acid groups of amino acids and carboxylic acid/amine groups of GQD-DPA, which is accompanied by GQD-DPA supported amino acids color alternation in the presence of Cu2+. However, the case is not the same for GQD-DPA supported high-concentrated amino acids (1 M). In fact, in high-concentrated cysteine, some of the cysteine molecules are remained free in the solution. In the presence of Hg2+, the free unreacted cysteine molecules react with Hg2+ though thiol-ion-thiol interactions and no agglomeration was occurred in the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) molecule. Therefore, the no color alternation was revealed for the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) in the presence of Hg2+. Moreover, high-concentrated cysteine (1 M) cannot detect Cu2+. The reason might be attributed to the chelating of Cu2+ molecules by the free cysteine molecules, thus, no accumulations were occurred for GQD-DPA molecules. Unlike Cu2+, and Hg2+, the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) sensing solution can detect Fe3+. Regarding to the three binding capacities of Fe3+, this ion can interact with three thiol/amine groups in the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) system, causing to GQD-DPA molecules agglomeration. Therefore, as illustrated in Fig. 6, GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1 M) can detect Fe3+ in environmental sample. Moreover, Fig. 6 indicates Cu2+ detection by GQD-DPA supported arginine (1 M) sensing solution. In fact, since amine and thiol groups are participated in the chelating process of Cu2+ by DPA, the presence of arginine, holders extra structural amine groups, can amplify GQD-DPA agglomeration. Therefore, the color of the GQD-DPA supported arginine (1 M) sensing solution is altered to blue in the presence of Cu2+.

In conclusion, we suggested cysteine amino acid for ion detection on µPADs, owing to the chelating behavior of cysteine towards Cu2+, and binding tendency for Fe3+, caused to amplify colorimetric detection of these ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Fe3+ and Cu2+, using μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 reveals the optical micrographs for the Fe3+, and Cu2+ detection assays performed on μPADs. As illustrated in Fig. 7, the optical images on the μPAD was captured under natural light, in which the color alternations of the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (Fig. 7) were evaluated through the μ-channels, both via non-capillary sensing. Ion detection on the μPADs was performed by GQD-DPA supported cysteine solution. In this case, the color alternation was appeared in the presence of Cu2+, and Fe3+. In fact, the size of the GQD-DPA supported cysteine (1.7 nm) is more than that for the bare GQD-DPA (0.8 nm). Thus, GQD-DPA supported cysteine reached to the higher dimensions in the presence of Cu2+. In comparison with the non-amino acid supported state, leading to occur color alternation on the paper. With the same reason, in the presence of Fe3+, owing to the high tendency for thiol groups to interact with the iron metal ion, cysteine (and even some of the DPA molecules) are detached from GQD particles, leading to reduce the dimension of GQD-DPA supported cysteine to 0.64 nm, which is even lower than the non-cysteine supported GQD-DPA particles. Thus, Fe3+ can also be detected on the μPADs in the presence of cysteine (Fig. 7).

To the best of our knowledge, the proposed technique for selective Cu2+ detection among other different metal ions in environmental fluids, human urine samples, and on paper-based μPADs is completely innovative and was not previously reported. The results revealed our new technique for the colorimetric recognition of copper ion, in both urine and aqueous samples, is selective, simple, fast, sensitive, quantitative, and cost-effective which can be detected by naked eye through color alternation with admirable stability. Moreover, we proposed to apply amino acids, human biological substances, to recognize Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. In addition, the enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor. Our proposed sensing probe can detect Cu2+ ion with the sensitivity of 0.1 ppm in humans’ urine samples. Thus, the proposed sensing probe has the potential for in advance detecting of low concentration of Cu2+ in physiological medium to prevent the diseases which are raised from copper overdoses. In addition, the enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

We proposed a sensitive, selective, accurate, cost-effective, and simple colorimetric method for Cu2+ detection in both environmental and biological samples. In this study, GQD-DPA was offered. DPA is a chelator for Cu2+, thus it can efficiently detect Cu2+ through simple colorimetric and fluorometric approaches. Further, regarding to raise the accuracy of the sensing system, we used several amino acids, which showed the same results of Cu2+ detection. Likewise, Fe3+, and Hg2+ can also be detected in the presence of thiol-contained cysteine amino acids. We offered µPADs for in situ and on-time sensing of Fe3+, and Cu2+ on the paper-based sensor, which is a sensitive, cost-effective, and accurate system. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed technique for selective Cu2+ detection among different metal ions in aqueous, human urine samples, and on paper-based μPADs is completely innovative and was not previously reported.
